text:
# Without the right figures, green IT remains an illusion

[Analysis](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/analyses/index.html)

[Cross-sector](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/alle-sectoren/index.html)

10/11/2025 • 9 minutes reading

[Julia Krauwer](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/authors/julia-krauwer/index.html)

Companies that use computing power and data storage in the cloud and want to green their IT must know where profits can be made. However, cloud providers do not yet share all figures on energy use and CO₂ emissions, and each does so in a different way.

## Interview with Julia Krauwer at BNR Nieuwsradio

Listen to the fragment with Sector Banker Julia Krauwer about the importance of good data in making IT more sustainable.

As a result, IT teams do not know where the most sustainability gains can be achieved and they cannot measure the impact of optimizations. For the time being, however, there are no legal obligations and mandatory standards that determine what information cloud companies must make available to their own customers.

## Limit negative environmental impacts

Companies worldwide are moving their IT activities to the cloud, and both the development and use of artificial intelligence (AI) are driving the demand for data center capacity. According to an [estimate](https://www.iea.org/reports/energy-and-ai/energy-supply-for-ai) from the International Energy Agency, global electricity consumption from data centers in 2024 was around 460 terawatt hours (TWh), which is equivalent to the annual consumption of countries such as Sweden or Argentina. By 2030, the agency expects this to more than double to 1000 TWh. About 40 percent of that power will come from renewable sources, but almost half will still require coal and gas-fired power stations. The CO2 footprint of digitalization will therefore remain substantial for the time being.

metadata.source_title: Without the right figures, green IT remains an illusion

text:
Data centers have become increasingly efficient in recent years. This has a lot to do with economies of scale; A large data center can distribute the energy for cooling servers – a relatively large share of power consumption – over a large number of customers. Progress has also been made with new, more energy-efficient cooling techniques and software to manage the indoor climate. However, users of AI and cloud services, which determine the demand for data center capacity, do not have sufficient tools in practice to reduce the adverse environmental effects of their IT efforts.

## Measuring is knowing

“Greening IT always starts with the right information,” says Wiebren van der Zee, specialist in the field of green IT at ABN AMRO. "Consider data about energy and water use, emissions, and use of raw materials. In our IT organization we collect this data for the workplaces of our employees, i.e. the laptops and screens, but also for our own data centers and the cloud services that we use."

## Guidelines for sustainable digitalization

metadata.source_title: Without the right figures, green IT remains an illusion

text:
## Guidelines for sustainable digitalization

Together with various organizations from the National Coalition for Sustainable Digitalization (NCDD), ABN AMRO has developed the [Sustainable IT Impact Assessment](https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fcoalitieduurzaamdigitalisering.nl%2Fsustainabl e-it-impact-assessment-siia%2F&data=05%7C02%7Cjulia.krauwer%40nl.abnamro.com%7C9948116aa7d649b3c17508de1082ebd8%7C3a15904d3fd 94256a753beb05cdf0c6d%7C0%7C0%7C638966351662129336%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiO iJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=Yz6QXd%2FJKDj3vTjpdic9%2FGPln8C8x2QH6hBRajvDbgc%3D&reserved=0) (SIIA) developed. This tool offers practical tools and extensive background information to start making IT more sustainable. The advice is based, among other things, on the concrete experiences of companies and governments.

For several years, major cloud providers have been trying to meet this information need through CO₂ dashboards that allow their customers to monitor the emissions from their digital efforts. Willem Jan Groenewoud, CEO of software company Ab Ovo, welcomes this wave: “It is good that they provide more transparency than a few years ago. Measuring is knowing.” The company is strongly committed to making the software it develops for the 'mission-critical' processes of its customers more sustainable. “We assume a scenario in which energy may be rationed, and we therefore design software that uses as little energy as possible.”

metadata.source_title: Without the right figures, green IT remains an illusion

text:
The cloud world is much more transparent than a few years ago, but there is still a lot to be said about the information provided. Not all necessary data is provided, and measurement methods and level of detail of the data vary widely between providers.

## Paper reality

It starts with the way cloud companies report their own organization-wide emissions. According to an analysis by the British newspaper The Guardian, the own data centers of the largest tech companies emitted almost [eight times](https://www.theguardian.com/technology/2024/sep/15/data-center-gas-emissions-tech) more than – added up – reported. This analysis included data from 2020 through 2022.

The difference can be explained by the measurement method. This is often 'market-based'. This means that providers calculate their CO₂ emissions based on the number of purchased certificates for green energy or long-term purchase contracts for energy from, for example, a wind farm. However, such constructions say nothing about the actual power mix at the data center location. “It is a paper reality,” says Van der Zee of ABN AMRO. “Green energy is administratively transported all over the world.”

For example, a cloud provider can purchase 'guarantees of origin' for a hydroelectric power plant in Norway and allocate them for accounting purposes to a server park in Ireland, while that park partly runs on electricity from coal-fired power stations. On paper the footprint is zero, but in reality there are indeed CO₂ emissions due to the use of fossil energy.

## Average flow mix

Under the European sustainability law CSRD, Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive in full, market-based reporting alone is no longer enough. On the one hand, organizations should be encouraged to purchase green energy and it is good that they provide insight into these efforts, on the other hand, the market-based measurement method gives too rosy a picture of the actual emissions from electricity generation. That is why the CSRD also requires companies to report 'location-based'. The average power mix in the region where the company or data center purchases power is the starting point.

Both measurement methods are also reflected in the emissions dashboards of cloud companies. However, the location-based figures are not displayed equally prominently in all cases. At Amazon, the market-based emissions data is pushed forward, and users have to click through to gain insight into the location-based figures. Microsoft even completely omits location-based emissions in its dashboard.

## Location-based reporting

metadata.source_title: Without the right figures, green IT remains an illusion

text:
## Location-based reporting

Location-based emissions are calculated by multiplying electricity consumption in kilowatt hours (kWh) by the local 'emissions factor'. This shows how much CO2 equivalent is released on average when producing a kWh of electricity. In regions with wind turbines or hydroelectric power stations, the emission factor is lower than in regions where electricity is mainly processed in coal or gas power stations. The location-based measurement method is an average and does not require insight into fluctuations in the energy mix per day or hour.

## Goodwill of suppliers

In the world of emissions dashboards, there are no mandatory standards, and customers of cloud services are in practice dependent on the goodwill of their cloud providers in unlocking information. As a result, the level of detail varies greatly. For example, most offer monthly figures, but the update speed varies. Some providers do this constantly, others are months behind. There is also variation in terms of fineness; Sometimes emissions are only shown per organization or region, other times they can also be monitored down to the level of individual services or projects.

In addition, not all dashboards include scope 3 emissions in the calculations. While scope 1 and 2 in this case concern the use of fossil energy and fossil-generated electricity to keep the data centers running, scope 3 concerns the more indirect emissions. These are caused, for example, by the production and disposal of IT equipment, and the construction of data centers and offices. The cloud providers that report at scope 3 level do not do so consistently; While one is limited to hardware production, the other also includes running the company restaurant, for example.

Boavizta, an initiative of dozens of European IT companies and consultancy organizations, has developed a clear way to calculate the climate impact of digital infrastructure. Now each cloud provider charges in its own way, which creates an incomplete picture and makes it difficult to compare figures between suppliers. For example, Boavizta makes data available on the environmental impacts of server production and transportation, which companies can use to more thoroughly calculate the carbon footprint of their IT activities. Resilio also offers such data, in combination with advice, but as a commercial service.

## Insight for development teams

The cloud dashboards are often primarily focused on insight and reporting. It is positive that some providers also provide concrete advice on how companies can reduce their emissions. For example, Microsoft and Google provide suggestions for scaling down unused capacity.

metadata.source_title: Without the right figures, green IT remains an illusion

text:
The cloud dashboards are often primarily focused on insight and reporting. It is positive that some providers also provide concrete advice on how companies can reduce their emissions. For example, Microsoft and Google provide suggestions for scaling down unused capacity.

However, it is difficult for organizations with a rich IT landscape to gain insight into emissions at application level. Software generally consists of multiple components that are spread across projects, regions or services. As a result, the direct effects of changes that development teams implement – ​​'lighter' code, or a more efficient architecture – are easily left out of the picture. “This is why we have developed our own AI-driven tooling for this,” says Groenewoud of software company Ab Ovo.

## Use less energy

According to Max Schulze, founder of the Sustainable Digital Infrastructure Alliance (SDIA), the focus on emissions is actually insufficient. “We should optimize for less energy use, for fewer data centers. We live on a continent with limited space and resources,” he says.

The other experts also recognize that green IT starts with insight into energy use. “The emissions from a data center are not a pure measuring instrument,” says Groenewoud of Ab Ovo. “We first map the electricity used in kilowatt hours at function level. Only then do we look at the location of the data center and the energy mix at that location.”

However, none of the cloud providers provide insight into the number of kWh their customers consume. Illogical, says Schulze of the SDIA, because you always have to start with the efficiency of your product. “When you buy a car, you want to know how many kilometers you can drive on a liter of fuel to get an idea of ​​the vehicle's efficiency. Only then do you translate this into the number of grams of CO2 equivalent per kilometer.”

## Alternative solutions

Various parties are filling the gap created by the limited information provision of cloud providers. For example, there is Cloud Carbon Footprint (CCF), an open source tool that reads the actual use of cloud services and converts it into kilowatt hours (kWh) and CO2 emissions. This data is also made available hourly, allowing IT teams to see, for example, when applications are used most and which systems are left running unnecessarily at night.

metadata.source_title: Without the right figures, green IT remains an illusion

text:
Although it can be a great starting point for organizations to gain additional insight, there is no guarantee with open source services such as CCF that it will be updated on time and regularly. This guarantee is available for commercial parties, and various paid services have been launched in recent years, including Greenpixie.

## Data from the source

All services, from cloud-native dashboards to externally developed solutions, are based on assumptions about the energy use of the servers and the digital services running on them. That is why project NADIKI, an initiative of SDIA together with the University of Stuttgart and data center operator Kolo, goes all the way back to the source: the data center. It collects live measurement data via sensors and links it to the current CO2 intensity of the power grid. “We have sensors everywhere, from the power supply of the entire data center, to rack and server level,” says Schulze. This information is used to collect energy usage and emissions for specific workloads.

Schulze: “This is the first time that energy and emissions are measured end-to-end.” The project is still in the pilot phase, but shows that there is room for improvement compared to current methodologies.

## Keep communicating

When emissions dashboards were first launched, they were received as a nice and much-needed feature. Some of the cloud service providers have great ambitions in providing transparency and see it as a source of differentiation. However, the pace varies widely across the board.

According to Van der Zee of ABN AMRO, organizations must make their wishes known to their IT suppliers. “They need to continue to communicate about the type of information they need.” As long as the information provision is not optimal, the gap will be filled via open source tools and the services of specialized software companies.

There are also standards, such as those of Boavizta, and the Greenhouse Gas Protocol also has IT-specific guidelines. However, there are no real obligations, and this poses unnecessary challenges for organizations that want to green their IT. Regulations can provide relief and ensure that all players report in the same way.

## Read more in the technology sector

Technology, Media & Telecom (TMT) is one of the fastest growing sectors. Aided by the increasing use of software, algorithms and sensors, this sector is the basis for the digital innovation of other sectors.
